title: Self-similar asymptotics in the decay problem for the Volterra lattice with zero boundary condition
authors: V. E. Adler, B. I. Suleimanov
abstract: The article is devoted to the problem of decay of initial stationary state for the Volterra lattice with zero boundary condition. We show that this process is asymptotically self-similar and calculate the propagation velocity of the decay wave, the leading terms of the asymptotics and corrections, in the main and transition sectors of the wave.
summary: This work provides a deep theoretical analysis of the long-time decay behavior of solutions on the Volterra lattice. By proving self-similarity and employing sophisticated techniques involving Painlevé equations, it calculates the precise asymptotic profile of the decaying state. This is crucial for understanding wave dynamics in nonlinear discrete systems.
main_problem: The research focuses on determining the self-similar asymptotics for the decay of an initial stationary state within the Volterra lattice model under zero boundary conditions.
main_result: The process is shown to be asymptotically self-similar, allowing for explicit calculation of the propagation velocity and the leading terms of the asymptotic profile using advanced matching techniques.
method: The analysis employs formal asymptotic expansions (FAS) in inverse powers of time, requiring a matching procedure near the front edge that involves solving equations related to the second Painlevé equation (P-II).
model_or_system: The primary model is the Volterra lattice ($\dot{u}_n = u_n(u_{n+1} - u_{n-1})$), which models an idealized food chain. Generalizations involve varying the nonlinear coupling function $r(u)$ and considering non-zero boundary conditions.
key_observables: Propagation velocity of the decay wave ($2v$), leading asymptotic functions ($f_0, g_0$), and higher-order correction coefficients ($f_j, g_j$).
important_parameters: Initial data parameters ($\alpha$), time evolution direction ($t 	o +\infty$ vs. $t 	o -\infty$), and boundary condition strength ($ eta$).
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on the assumption of asymptotic self-similarity, requiring the use of matching techniques when standard series expansions fail near the wave front.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate numerical solutions showing decay wave formation for various parameters (e.g., $\alpha=1$ or $\alpha=1/3$), and comparison between theoretical inner and outer asymptotic expansions using stretched variables ($z$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the self-similar nature of the solution, deriving leading order ODEs for coefficients, solving these systems by relating them to Painlevé transcendents (P-II), and finally applying matching techniques to determine the full asymptotic profile for $t 	o \pm\infty$.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical treatment of nonlinear lattice decay problems using advanced methods like Painlevé analysis and asymptotic matching is highly relevant to understanding wave propagation in complex, non-integrable physical systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Solution for α = 1, at several moments t. Here and in the following figures, the red dots correspond to odd n and the blue dots to even n.
Figure 3. Numerical solution and the first correction term for α = 3.
Figure 4. Numerical solution and the first correction term for α = 1/3.
Figure 5. Left: bipartite kink (3.1) for t = 0, α = 1/3 and δ = 0. Right: limiting position of the inversion point for α = 1/3.

Figure 7. Solution near n = 2t in stretched variables z and v, for t = 100. Left: black curves are the graphs of the leading terms of the inner expansions. Right: black curves are the graphs of the leading terms plus first corrections.
